mission.title: Install and Configure Radius on Kubernetes
mission.description: Radius is a cloud-native application platform that simplifies app development and management for teams building cloud-native applications. Installing Radius allows you to leverage its features for team collaboration, infrastructure recipes, and cloud-neutral deployments across various environments.

steps.title: Add the Radius Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Radius Helm repository to your local Helm setup to access the Radius charts.
```bash
helm repo add radius https://p2p-org.github.io/avs-helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Radius using Helm
steps.description:
Next, install the Radius application platform using Helm. This command will create a new namespace called 'radius' and deploy Radius in that namespace.
```bash
helm install radius radius/radius --version v0.54.0 --namespace radius --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
After installation, verify that the Radius pods are running successfully in the 'radius' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace radius
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure that your Radius deployment has appropriate resource limits, you can edit the deployment configuration. Use the following command to set CPU and memory limits:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment radius --namespace radius
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: '500m'
#     memory: '512Mi'
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the Radius application, set up port forwarding to the service. This will allow you to interact with Radius locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/radius --namespace radius 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Radius will result in the Radius pods running in the 'radius' namespace, and you will be able to access the application through port 8080 on your local machine. Ensure that the resource limits are configured as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add radius https://p2p-org.github.io/avs-helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install radius radius/radius --version v0.54.0 --namespace radius --create-namespace

kubectl edit deployment radius --namespace radius
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: '500m'
#     memory: '512Mi'

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Radius deployment, use the following command to uninstall the Helm release. This will delete all resources associated with the release in the specified namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall radius --namespace radius
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent volumes created by Radius, you can remove them with the following commands. Ensure to check for any remaining resources before deletion.
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace radius
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the 'radius' namespace to clean up all remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been deleted successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace radius
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up your current Radius configuration and data. You can export the current configuration using the following command:
```bash
helm get values radius --namespace radius > radius-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, and then upgrade the Radius installation to the desired version. Replace `<new-version>` with the version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade radius radius/radius --version <new-version> --namespace radius
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Radius pods are running the new version successfully. Check the status of the pods in the 'radius' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace radius
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your Radius pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue. Use the following command to view the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace radius
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Radius service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check if the service is running and the correct ports are exposed:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace radius
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set for the deployment. You can view the current configuration with:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment radius --namespace radius
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error indicating that the Helm release was not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace and release name. List all releases in the namespace with:
```bash
helm list --namespace radius
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
